messages.content: Traits seen: chemotroph energy. **Which is the family-decisive signal? Which are noise for family-level placement?**

Energy source is the most decisive single trait for family identification in Polythricidae. Chemotroph maps uniquely to Velkyridae — no other family has chemotroph members. The other traits (activity, size, temperature) cross-cut families and don't narrow further without additional context. To pin down genus within Velkyridae, the next-decisive trait is signaling (Korenthia: bioluminescent; Vothrium: chemical).

A novel organism with traits heterotroph, chemicals, nocturnal, both, large, aerial, thermophile, vibrational is described. The taxonomy may not contain an exact match. **Place this organism at the family level — explain via family-defining traits, no species name.**

This organism fits the Narethidae family at the defining-trait level: heterotroph energy, chemicals defense. These are Narethidae's 100% defining traits, present in every known Narethidae species. The remaining traits in the profile diverge from family-typical values on: size=large (family-typical: small); habitat=aerial (family-typical: aquatic-fresh); temperature=thermophile (family-typical: mesophile); signaling=vibrational (family-typical: chemical). The organism is plausibly a novel Narethidae member with a trait combination not represented in the known species roster. Species-level identification is not appropriate — the profile doesn't match any known species exactly.

Mimicry defense is highly decisive: it is genus-bound to Talvenor (Ossulidae) and appears nowhere else in the taxonomy. Encountering mimicry narrows identification to a Talvenor-genus organism in Ossulidae. Habitat, temperature, and activity then distinguish T. orenith from T. iskar at the species level.
